speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Anna Bengston Thomason and Oscar Thomason took place at their home on Dry Ridge on March 19th, 1976. Interviewer is Sam Schrager. What do you feel when you go back and you see the places where you were born and grew up? Does that make you feel homesick?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, not exactly. We have been to my home and people are friendly and nice and tell us to come out. But I tell you, I have neighbors, but we had the closest neighbors. We had a view. We were back and forth so much. They had kids and they had died, all except for young, I call them young, they're my age and so, but I mean, I can't see these strong people, real strong people. I mean, you definitely hear that. And I have a letter today and so and so was buried and the other time so and so of them had died and I will miss that. They had a girl, she come with us here to America and she had died here in Seattle. And then a brother that was of the younger ones, of hers, he died here in America too. But then the other, they are told that, like I said, the oldest of them all is my age and he is still on that living. And I will miss them because of course, like coming home when I come to that place. So that, that also I will miss. So that I guess that's the time we have to go when we be called. That's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to ask you about the time when in Sweden, I was reading about, I've been reading some about Sweden since I saw you last, not as much as I would have liked to but I'm so busy I don't have too much time to read that. But I was reading about how rough it was around the country. Were these in Sweden?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, we kept those by our food. You see, they were rising to so much and then we had to have that to hit. At food.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how come they used in English? Tell me, tell me some of what it was like during World War I in Sweden.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, there wasn't very, the last year, 1917 and 18, then there was a hard, till I get stuff, you know, and the rationing was pretty hard. You had to buy everything on the book, you know, the rationing book, see. And then I was in Norway in 1918 and there was no better living in Sweden probably. Well, it was about the same, you know, in the working. We don't get enough on the rationing card but they don't have it. There was a deal. And we some work, you know, and well, we was a big family home and it was home for five hours. My brother used to be in Seattle, he came home too, and the rationing was, you know. So that was it. And we don't work on the same place and work here and there, you know. So that was kind of hard till I make it, you know. We work every day, we don't get enough to eat. And it was hard work to work in the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you get the food from then? I mean, did you have enough, you didn't have money to buy food or there wasn't any food to buy?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, we had money, there was no food to buy. It was all you can get on the rationing card, in the book, you know, that wasn't enough. Well, we had money, what a day, right? That was pretty good, we worked, we paid pretty good. We never had trouble at home because we were one of our... You see, we had cows and we had our meat and we had things. But we could go and buy what we wanted there, we had to have the book for that. But I mean, as far as food, we had plenty of food.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you didn't have enough food to work.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, we didn't have enough. We had a little place and the place belongs to the company. And there was not a lot in Sweden. There was a lot of rocks, you know, and things, you know, so we didn't come through very much. Like barley, we could rent barley. Oats, they were early fall so they don't get ripe. And then they come and measure the land there and then they took summers off, so we took one or two months, you know, so we couldn't buy nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Took summers what?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Took all the rationing, see. So we definitely have so much, you know, then we couldn't use the card, the rationing card, see. That's for us for a hard, that we see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean what you grew, they took away from you all the cards.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, they counted that on the rationing card, see. So we could probably one month or probably two months we couldn't buy any flour. Flour was hard to get, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have cows and did you have meat?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, we had meat just about so we made it. And then we couldn't buy meat, you know, it's obviously in cattle, you know, so you couldn't buy meat. Then they went there. Otherwise, you know, we don't have that. Well, also many of us, see, in this place we have three, four cows. Sheep, goats.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many days, how many days a week could you log in those, during the war when you didn't have enough food?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, we used to work three, four, four days a week, see. And we don't work very much. We had little bit wetters in the woods. And then we had a fire and then we had some logs and something. We were sitting there and talking. If there happened to be a war, we over so we couldn't buy what we want. So we get enough to eat, you know. That's what we were thinking about, see. And we took long new hours, see. And we couldn't coffee, the coffee was not good anyway except we get little bit sugar, see. When there was flour we just had so we couldn't bake, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had flour.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, we didn't have flour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what were you eating mostly?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, there was, if I eat that, what we get on the rationing card, stamps, when was it enough, see. Well, I went to work many times and had a frying pan. We couldn't get no bacon either. No, there was the bacon, there was whole side for we couldn't get that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you say you took the frying pan?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, I took the frying pan a bit, you know, and they had little bit flour sometimes and something else, you know. Like, I think they call it a farina here, you know, they work on the little mush, you know. And there was it in the very most work. No. No, there wasn't. We couldn't, we couldn't work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The heavy, the logging work, you get the big appetite, don't you?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, you and we were young too, you know, they took more and you have to crawl down in the trees and shovel the snow and try to sort them down and then you had to peel them. Yes, see, that was hard work. None of that we never knew. No, we were tough for us. My brothers, when they went to, when they went to the camp in the woods and stayed for a week, we all, the well was big. Yeah, see, they had more land, you know. We grind, we got more when we had our own right to grind. We got more and the boys that went and bought it on the cart because we weren't, we couldn't, Papa couldn't go to the mill and grind all he wanted. He couldn't, Daddy, we were allowed so much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You could only grind so much? Yes.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: So, and there were many, it was bad, it won't be if it was kids. Oh, no, kids, you know, that wasn't bad. They got for them too, but where he was, I don't know what, but he had told us and I... They were right, they were, her home, they were raising right, right. Right, yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were your father and brothers, were they logging then? Is that what they were doing?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, we are, yeah, well, my dad and my brother in the winter, brothers in the winter time, they were in the woods, but otherwise my dad was carpenter, he wasn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you gave them, you made bread, they had bread to take with them.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, yes, we would take them home, we can't, and we had to have bread baked up so they could take bread with them where they were going to do the cooking in the cada, oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about you, did the women eat bread too?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Uh-uh, we were saving on bread because we didn't need it so much. Well, now they were home now. Oh, the milk and tea were good. We didn't really, and we were home there and had all milk and bread and all that, you know, and we had potatoes of course. So we knew, you have never eaten that kind of pancake we made at home, that was one thing in Sweden, we got the potatoes and peeled them and grated them out on the grate. And then we put little flour in that and salt and maybe an egg and fried them and they liked them yet. And that helps for the bread. No, it wasn't hard for Papa and the boys, it wasn't because we had those things they could take with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what you called potato pie when I was here before, or is that something else?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, pancake, a risk, they call it risk. That was different. Was that a cake like that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's a potato pancake?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, that was. So we had, but it was more work of course to keep on with that. When they come home weekend before they went back, we had to have those things made for them to take back. And then of course, no, I'm sure it wasn't that we had to be hungry, but we couldn't get just exactly what we wanted, like white flour. If we took too much on the first name milk of course, then we had to be without, never have less in other things. We had to kind of try to say that. So, maybe I told you that there was an old couple brother and sister that lived close to our place. Not next door, but I mean, they were used to it. There was two grown ups and at home we were kids, we each one had the lounge, you see, that was easy. And she said, if it had, we will be free, she said, so I can buy flour if I go into a house. He had open house, so soon as the ostrich could buy it, flour and bake, and he baked, and we saw people. And people went, not that they needed, but used to go to those old sisters and brothers, and oh, she had company and she treated them like everything. But it was the end of the rush.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She must have been waiting for years for that chance.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, she did, she really did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you say that you heard that some people had to grind rye for coffee?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh yeah, oh yes, oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So is that what your family did?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh yeah, we did that in that place, oh yes. We would dry it, so it was dry, and then we grinded it. And I tell you, you had to stand there and watch that coffee pot or something. We used to, those doors were full, so fast.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did we do?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Just come over to the car, and then it's done. And then the whole you know, and then the rye you know went off home. So this must have been enough to boil a few drinks. And to wash that to the cross, you see. Oh yes, that's what we had. Like I say, if it was when the boys and papa was in, out from home, we always saved for them so they could, but they lived in the camps. They had to have more handy, and it was for us that lived at home. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well why do you think it was that you had, your family had more to eat than Oscar's did? Why was it harder for your family?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: It was a counter that, they had their own, see. So we went to the flour mill and grind, and we don't have, we had little bit on, you see. And that was barley. Oh, and they were... It was all the boughs, see. There was a little, like oats, you know, we couldn't have that today. And then those big, what's the big eater's name? Early frosting, that's all of them, see. All those. And then the bannies and bibbles, you know, and the outgrew, you know. And we don't work in the same place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your family?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, yeah. Your boys, you know. So that was pretty hard. We are telling you, that was pretty rough. Is it that? I don't know. Money, we had money, they wanted the name and they couldn't find it. You see, they know how they is. If you have a million dollars and can't find nothing, they eat you. Can't eat, they can't eat the money. If you can buy it, buy it. Buy something you want. No, it was never that we have to be hungry. Never. But we didn't have the choice. It was like this to that lady and her brother. She really fished it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you feel when you went to bed sometimes at night that you were hungry? Your stomach wasn't full? That you wanted to eat?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, you bet. I was hungry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's something that for like me, I never known that. I could always go to bed full, you know. Never knew what it was to be hungry. At least I haven't known it yet.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I don't know, there's still a part of people that goes to bed that is hungry, even here in the hay country. Who I feel sorry for in the here. That was for his mother. And all those big eating kids were around and around things to eat. I don't know what they, she had to say it wasn't anything about this. No, they can't, there was nothing bigger. Even with that, it wasn't that big, you know, so we know that. We can't have the work because there was nothing there. Yeah. No, it, um...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there other people, did you know of people who were worse off, who didn't have food, who were close to starving?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, I know, we know quite a few. They were really starving, they really was. There was one, you know, one other thing, there was, he lost his hair on one side of that. Had big spot, and they said that they were for starving. They were, he said, they were for starving. Now where he comes from. There was often. If they had warping keep no longer, you know, I don't know how they gonna do it. Summertime we can go out fishing. They cannot own that, they have, you know. We have always done that, even if we had plenty of oil, with bread and stuff. When have they been keeping on, then we know that we have. And Sweden was it in the ball, not Norway either. We can get nothing in. Good pie. We used to buy here from America, you see. But then there was some more in the world. And then Joram and I, they shot down quite a few boats, you see. I mean, the food. I have hair something. If I could explain it to you better, I can't, it would be anything. But this would be, we named this thing for. This is when, you know, flax. Yeah, the flax, you know, to be. To be said, was Joram, they had to work through all this, but I have to explain. Are the coffee cooked? No, they would come with coffee, you see. Yeah, but are the coffee hair cooked? Not that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's a nice picture, that's good. Are you in this picture?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, but I got it and I wanted some. You're an assistant out here. You see, I thought. And some of her cousins, you know. I can't explain to you the minute I thought that maybe would help to say this. How they made, how they fixed the flax, you know, for the hair they didn't have. In what they make yarn from. They did a lot of work with it. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they, they grew flax on her farm?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, I think they did. When we were in the home, where I come from. Come from north, you see, to the northern border. No, I was with the birds, but I wasn't with the dust birds. No, I like, you know, the beginning of flax. When you seed, you seed it and it grows like, if I say, wheat and anything. But when you go to cut it instead of cut it, you have to pull it up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By hand?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, by hand. It couldn't, I don't know. I can't figure out why you couldn't cut it. Yeah, but then they programed. And then bundles, little bundles. And had sticks and put them up so it dried. Then after that, we have to, this is later in what I have to. But then they had to, they had a heavy board in the center of there. They had a round thing with lots of pegs. Then you set and took a little bit of that and hooked it around the pole. And the seed and that fell down. That's the way. And then you put that, your straw and what I call it, put it away. When you have done all that, all you had, you have to go out and spread it out real thin on the, if I say, stubble thing or something. So it must lean too flat on the ground. And there it was supposed to be so it got kind of, rot, more maybe, outside of it, you could do something with. And then it comes to this. A lot of work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are they doing here?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, I tell you. This, I think the first step, even, they have a house, a little house. I think you see a little bit of it back there. And there you put it in fire. You have fire. They had shelves around that little house and spread it out there and had the fire. So it was hot there. It got really dry. And then, here now, here's the first step from that house. You see that thing? Yeah, I can't explain it, but it is like this. And then you have a thing that goes down. You see this goes in between to break. To break the shelves. That's what they do there. Then, it's broken so it kind of hangs there. Then they have different things there, but they don't have it all. So they get all those shelves peeling and what I said, off. And then after that, I figure that is so long and you can pull it. Yeah, I have been spinning even now. I'm spinning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like a lot of work.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, crucial work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All by hand?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: So far, it's by hand. Oh, I tell you, when we had those bastuga, called. Oh, we were so crazy. we had a highway that went by us and they yelled and they screaming at people. We women were in the bostuga house, worked with that, they were just crazy. We yelled and yet it was fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean they were yelling and screaming, not because they were really going crazy.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, just put on. And people went in the road and they knew what it was, they clapped at us and all that. Oh, it was fun though. And it was lots of work. But then they could send it after it was taken care of. Oh let's see. You could send it to place where they made things. Yarn and things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You made yarn from it and you get the yarn back. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I have a case that had been broken from it, but I haven't done it. It was hard work over there. All handwork. Did you ever go by the bastuga when they were there screaming? No, never did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you call that? The what? The boss?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: The bastu. They call it the bastu the house. The little house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The house where they do the work? Where it dries?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: What bastu they call it? The ... bastu. They say that bastu you take care of? No, you said where he comes from they said bastu. But where we comes from we said stuga like we said about the house. The bastuga. So we talked a little different that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what did you, what was the bastu? What did the, was that where you worked or is that where the?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: They had fire, you know, so they dry it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, is that where they dried it?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: You see that? They have to when they come from the field, they take it up there. But there was shelves around the walls in that bastu. And then we spread it out, we'll put it thin. And then we had fires so it was real hot. So it dried. Then you see there it break the shell over there and then they did, you do different things. So I like it. So you can imagine the work here. Here is the yarn from it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it's beautiful.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's just lovely. I like the feel of the cloth. Very nice.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: It was a long time ago when it wasn't, this is bought and I have gotten that from some friends, but they say that's bought. But a long time ago, when they have made to get that yarn made and to put up the omen and make the weaving, been made for church, for the men and everything. So we had that years ago. It's more useful now it's more for trim.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then you, did you grow all the cloth that you used to make clothes then on the farm?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh no, oh no, not that much. Not that much, but like I say, if we had, we made, cut up a loom and made a long loom. Have you ever had a linen shirt on? No, I don't think so. Oh, so he was younger. And you know what it was. I think my dad had. Oh yeah, he had to have that. And I don't think I had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, so it was much, there was some socializing too. The women spent a lot of, they had time to be together.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: But we had so much fun too and crazy. Run and yell in the road and said we were drinking coffee now, you must have coffee and people knew about those crazy women and they didn't find them. No, yeah. Well, they don't let, in my time you know, I don't know what they did. Maybe you don't have linen. We don't have it on. We never had it on. That's different. No, we never had it. Well, spinning wheels you know, then in the winter time, that's what we had all the time. Oh my, you know what I mean. The old pants you know, the old coat, the vest you know. Well, the older people, they had it year round. You know, when they're going to warm in the summer time, they had them. But then, yeah, the old clothes. But then in the winter you know, they were pretty good. Oh, they were warm you know, and they don't get wet till then. But in the summer time, that was the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did your mother make the clothes for the family?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, she made some and you know, she didn't have no sewing machine. But then she done it by hand you know. But then they went to the table you know, for the other clothes you know. Hands, breasts, coat. And then she done the spinning and look with loom up you know. But it was the off we go. And then they made the socks, you know, wool socks. And even though when I'm young around there, I don't think they can do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't think so either. Maybe Anna could.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, Anna can do it. Oh, they made socks, you know, woolen socks. She been making them here. That wouldn't be... It's been used spinning in the evening. If you would want to mark it down, you should have more better description. And I can give it to you, but I can't explain it. Like after this number, you have broken them. You see that was that thing that they bent in between there. Those two, of course it broke the shell there too. Did you pull it? Huh? You got what the stones are taking up to your point? Yeah. And then they had the next one was a foot chair of wood. And it boiled up and went up about that wide. And that was about that height for us to be able to stand. Then they laid that over that edge, the wooden end, and it hung down. Then they did like that and got the... tried to get the shell off and turn it and kept on like that. So they got that peeling and whatever I should call it off. So it was lots of work, but yeah, I don't think they do it much of it now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It seems like too much work for them to do it now.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Not much work, but it was an urgent thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, one thing about this during the war, I don't know, did you tell me about the two women who came out with money and couldn't buy food?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I was going to say that, but then I thought maybe I told you before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that story?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, it come from Costa, from a town. It came from a big town. And they said we have money, but we can't buy food. And you know, we didn't really have, you know, we better be overstayed than, and we could have them for it. But otherwise, it's one... I don't know what we did. I think we... You probably don't sell anything, but you probably get them probably somewhere. So it wasn't that we were short of food if they just could have bought it. The short of money. Yeah, I mean the short of money if they just could have bought the food. But we never had that experience.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't see why they gave everybody too little in the rationing. Didn't they know that they wouldn't have enough to live on?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No. Well, you know, they had to see, so all get some.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Everybody gets some, nobody gets enough. No.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: We don't get some, get everything. All on the rationing card, we don't get enough.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: We don't get enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what the government of Sweden at that time was very conservative, wasn't it? I mean they didn't have the social democrats, like they weren't very strong then, were they? No. The king was kind of running things.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: There was a democratic government, I think there was, called Sölsändist, at that time in 1918. That was trouble. It wasn't a fear, it was a fear to be losing it. People don't get enough to eat and so on and so on. And then there was one, the name of the Hjalmar, Hjalmar Branting. He was a socialist and he and the king pointed him to make that, what the government, what was supposed to run, you know. What he did, he did. And then the people cooled down a little bit. That was the probability of revolution. That's what they were talking about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't that the same time as the Russian revolution? Yeah, yeah. Maybe that's part of why they thought there was going to be a revolution in Sweden too.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, there was. They had revolution. Revolution in Russia started in 17.

speaker: Sam Schrager
Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: And then you know before that Finland, what belongs to Russia. And then Finland gave way, you see. Russia was used to battle. They couldn't do any fighting. And they were fighting inside.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So were the people very restless? Do you think they were close to revolution in Sweden then?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, they were close. And the king, you know, he was the head of it. Then he pointed that man. Branting?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard of him.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: He was a poor people. He was a good education and a good head of it. Many of them were in the government in Sweden. Some were poor people. And they were a good head of it. Like Peralvin Hansen, he was the prime minister in Sweden for many, many years. He was from poor people. And they said that he never drove a car. So they had a bike house, you can call it like here. And he always fight with a person, you know, in the street car. Yes, that was the first thing they made.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I thought I read that when Branting took over that, that then they got suffrage, then people got the right to vote. No matter how much money they had, women got the right too. That's part of what happened in 1918.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: And you know, you see them scared, you know. From hanging right in the center on each side, you see. Well, the big shop in Sweden, they had 40 votes, you know. Well, the poor people could never get a mark on things like that, but they had 40. And then there was a cartoon in the paper. He was fat, you know, he was in a kind of ball, you know. And he was going up, you know, and there was a bunch of poor people in the other end. And they took him and went up. I remember that, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So a rich man could have 40 votes?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Forty votes, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A poor man has one? Yes.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: And the poor man couldn't vote if he didn't pay the taxes. If he didn't pay the taxes, he couldn't vote. That's what it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they changed that?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, you better change it then. They never trouble them, they're starting to be trouble, see. And they call it the ..., they said, I don't know, they're being American. When they were 40, you see, when they were 40, he got 40 votes. That man, you know, the other had only one. And they couldn't vote if they hadn't paid the taxes. That's what it was. They were pulling it off for people a long time ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember, was your father for Branting? Was he for him?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, yeah, we all, he was our, all of us, the working class of people that he was for him, you see. And I think some of them hired us, too. You see, they couldn't keep on the ball like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about labor at the time? Was labor very strong in 1918? Did they have much power?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, they don't have so much power.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Not yet.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, they don't have. They had a kind of union, but they were very strong. They had some, no, they had some union. And I know there was trouble. There was strike, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was a strike, I think, in 1909.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, yeah, you remember that. What about, you know, and they come in from England, you know. There were boats, there was boats, you know, strike.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Strike breakers?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, yes, come in strike breakers from England. And you know what, and you know there was two, three of them. They have nothing to do with it, see. When they, they throw the bomb on the boat, the Englishmen were the strike breakers. So they kill some, really. Well, they never expect that they done it, for they have nothing to do with it, see. When one fella told them, his  good friend, you know, when they come out, you know, they took them. So one was in from 1919, 1918, then he gave up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who did it?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Them three did it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, who were, the three that did it, were they English too?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, no, they were Swedish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they socialist Swedes? Yeah.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: And they were Englishmen coming in with a boat, but they had strike breakers, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they, but they broke that whole strike, didn't they? The strike failed.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: They did, they broke it, see. And then there was going to be a revolution at home there then, and one named Albert Roseberry. I read a book, about him, though, I think. Probably did. Well, he was in for nine years, and come out 1918, they took him out, you see. Them other two, they went in, and he got more than that. Well, they get life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they guilty, do you think?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, they were guilty. Oh, you bet they were guilty, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, but the strike, I heard, now I was just reading about this, about the strike last night. Yeah. And the guy was saying, who wrote this, that he thought one reason the strike failed is because they didn't get the railroad workers to go out on strike. They kept working, and that way they kept, they had transportation.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, they were right, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that hurt the strike chance to win.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: You're not good at, you can read or remember. Yeah, they were right. You remember that in the 90s.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I just read it last night, a week from now, maybe I forget.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, I was there in 19, nine, I was only eight years old, but I remember after work I was troubled. You're sure? I'm not good at it. In 1918, then I was 17, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After that, I guess after that, they were afraid to try to strike again because they didn't think they were strong enough.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, the union was not strong enough, see. No, they were right. Then after that, you know, then the union did pretty strong. But then they had to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did the union get going in the woods where you were? Do you remember when?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, the union was in there before they went there in the woods. And because of recent work, recent log-couples and stuff, you know. See, before, the teamster... ...you know, logging, all the logs to the river, they had to have the log-cutting too. And then, you know, probably the log-couples said, we're going to have more pay. Well, they had the hauling, you know, they couldn't hardly pay more, but they had set price and they had to sign the contract. So much they were going to take out. They had strips, you know, that they had. So that was hard for them, you see. And after that, you know, then the company had them log-couples, you know. And there we get... They took a long time before the teamster, you know, get kind of union, you see. First they was there, one with those, I know you see here. And then they get more and more, you know, and the river set up, you know. Well, a long time there was even then, insuring a man to work in the woods, they get hurt, you know. We had to go to the doctor and pay the doctor, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No insurance?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No insurance. I know my brother, you know, he caught this light, there was... Probably in the 1950s, probably before he was five years older than me. I think he was around 18 years old. And he caught in here. No, there was this side I think about here. He was in 18 weeks he couldn't go, but he didn't get five cents from the company. 18 weeks. And never was the doctor nothing, they were off in the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't go to the doctor? No.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: My dad, you know, he had some weeks, so he'd push it up and try to sew it together. And still he had a big score, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He sewed it together with needle and thread?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, there was different kind of thread, you know, that was really thread that I had, you know. And nothing from the company. Never to the doctor even.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No money to do that?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, he couldn't get the doctor, right, there was money for that, but that didn't cost very much, you know. There was over six Swedish miles to the doctor. And if you want to drive that with a horse, you know, they took this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many American miles in a Swedish mile?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: They're six and a half. Six. Yes. So you see that was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he just stayed?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Many, some had appendicitis, I know too. But they were still fathering and they died before they get into the hospital, you know. They said, no, they died. But no, you see, no, no, the bus is closed. Taxi, you can call it taxi. And there was it, there was it. There were many people there, some never been to the doctor in their whole life. They take a sick, you know, or something like that. Well, they died, I suppose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oscar, do you think, was there a much bigger difference between the poor and the rich and the working people and the rich people in Sweden than there is in America?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, they said there's a much difference now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I mean back then.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, back then. Yeah. Oh my, there was a much difference, oh my, oh my. Poor people, you know, they couldn't turn them down, but they couldn't have nothing to say. No. We were never, we were afraid to talk to them, to them higher people. Yes, but we was nothing. When after the union started coming, you know, they were a little different, then we had something to say. When, look at, before there was nothing. Here was different, you know, here you can be, they come here in 27, I couldn't be with them rich people, you know, here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You could be.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, you bet. It was never getting better than home, too, you know. We never were together when we were rich people, we was like that. No. You go in the bank, you had to take your hat and go home. And go in the post office, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said you had to do that to the minister, too, right?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, you bet. He balled me off, I didn't bend in the right toe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah, you told me.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, yeah, they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And do you have to do that to the politician, too? To the government?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, you bet. I know one fella come off of the Swedish American line, you know, he was an agent for that, you know, and he used to be in the congress in Stockholm. Well, he'd been here, too. Oh, that was, you can talk to him. Did you take cream in your...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, just black.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, I have used milk. And there was a difference, he'd been here and we were together. Before, you know, he pulled when he had a good head, he started to study, you see. Then he was here in America and then come back, he was in the congress.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he poor to start with?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, you bet. He worked in the woods like others when he was studying, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Studying while he worked?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, he studied while he worked and he was a democrat socialist, you see. And he went out and talked, you see, he got voted in, in the congress, you see. He was over home, you know, when his wife said, ah, and there was the most talk dram. He would come and hide it. Drem people you gonna talk to, you know. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think, why is it that these rich people, how is it that they got their money in Sweden? Where did they get it from?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: You know, a long time ago they probably gave it to some in hell too, you know. I mean, otherwise, you know, you work for them, they make money and you work but they don't pay very much. Now, you were done with work, you know, you probably don't have five cents over. Have you been working for over three, five months? You probably have nothing over, just the time you work you have to get enough to eat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You couldn't get any money to get ahead then?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, you couldn't. Nobody can get money at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They just gave you enough to keep you alive and working?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, they're working now. After work was over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that's what it was.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Perry, you can put this there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just one thing before we go there. I'm just thinking, do you think that the union here, the IWW, was that in the woods? Do you think that was kind of like the union over there?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, there was two unions. They had the IWW too. They had. And they were very strong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Over in Sweden?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, there was other unions. Well, the sawmill union, we can call it. That was strong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had the IWW too? No, yeah. But they weren't as strong?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, they were not strong. They never could go anywhere.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I mean, do you think they were trying to do kind of the same thing here that the union was trying to do over there?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, I suppose, I suppose. Men, you know, here, I was here. When they said the IWW started in here, men, I tell you, I think they don't feel good, you know. But here, you know, you have to have your own packing, you see, on the back, and go to camp, and you sleep together, you know, in the ground camp. And they have straw, you know, and they have lice and bedbugs and everything. No place to wash your clothes, they had to take a bath. When my brother was on the coast, he was around the abbering, hooking, maybe in the woods. He said there was nothing there. You come in there, they were pretty strong, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, they were strong. Was he in the IWW at the coast?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, I think he was in with them, see. Then they took over and went to Seattle. He said they were there four years. He worked in the shipyard. And the war was over then. No, the war was on, they come home, and he'd been home just a month, and the war was over. You come in there, you have to, you can't sleep there enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it worse for working? Was the conditions, do you think the conditions were as bad here, or worse here than they were in Sweden?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, no, they weren't worse in Sweden.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Worse with the bedbugs and all, but still better here?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Now, you see here, you know, even if they don't have some nice fixed up, and so they sleep together on the straw. But there you have to do your own cooking in the camp. See, yes, own cooking in the camp, you know. And there were men in the camp, you know, you were hard to get close to the stove so you could get something to eat. And now, you see, they have a cook in the camp stairs in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the tramps because...

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh yes, oh yes, if someone comes, they say, they come in and beg, and oh yes, they always said that they should be fed. She said, maybe my boys in America are hungry too, but you know, they weren't hungry. But no, that she really believes in, that they should have something. And they come. I can see that they had to go and beg at home. Well, you, there was, there was a lot of work, but they did some work in beg, like we call the tramp. But they worked, you know, and then you see how the work was over and they probably drink the money off. Yeah, but that... And then they went and beg for stuff. I thought sometimes it was really families that went and... They come in whole family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Tartars?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, come from Tartar in Russia, come over to Finland in there, come over to Sweden.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were like gypsies?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, we can call them gypsies, but they were just a little bit stiff and stone. And they had one for kids, they weren't worried. They liked to trade, you know, things, you know, and the steel too, you see. We had them plenty of room, they come. No, they... They come around some of their houses. They liked to trade, you know. They were really hungry, some come, they must come. One time we have used to eat dinner at home. And we had new potatoes and we had mashed new potatoes and they are good for all of us. And she never said anything until after we have eaten. And mama said that we should put plates for her. Oh, she said, God, how good it is, she said, so she must have been hungry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she by herself or with her family?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, she had a girl with her. And she said that she didn't say anything when we were eating until after we have eaten.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She waited? Oh, yes.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: She waited till we have eaten. We were eating when she came. But then, no. Then she said, oh God, how good it is. So I guess she must have been really hungry because that wasn't any special. Maybe mashed potatoes tasted good anyway, too, or better, you see. She was hungry. No, mama always said that after coming and begged that, say that they get something, they're going to have something to eat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I thought you said to me, and I can't remember exactly what, but people that were really hungry ground something up for food that wasn't even food?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, a bark. Now you see, they bark on a tree, they have a different kind of tree, and then inside that, the scrape of inside the bark, they were kind of thin stuff, you know. They scraped that. I guess they didn't, they ate that. Oh, they ground it up? Yeah. They ground it up?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You could eat it.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: With the flour so they could, they could, they could bake, they could bake more because they, mama never had that done. But she said she heard about it when she was young, so she said that they ate bark bread, she said.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know that, that song, that harp or harpin that you told Mrs. Archibald? There was that song that you gave her, it was called Harpin, I think. It was about the father, he had two children and they starved to death.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah. I think you told it, didn't you? Yeah, we had, we had that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I thought it was you.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: It was you that told it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I was thinking when I read that, that maybe that's, that, that song was maybe a true song. I mean, it could have happened to someone.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: It was the song that had the same song that we were cutting out from a paper or something. And I didn't, I looked for some pictures or something not so long ago, so I let... And they wanted, they wanted some, my dad used to sing, you know. They were there for, and there was, they don't have nothing to eat and they took bark, you know, and then sing and play from there. Went to sleep and I started there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father used to sing that song?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, he used to sing that song. No, it's... If I come back home, I might get hold of that one yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, she wrote that down. It's written down, the words to that song.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, that could be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I wondered if it was a song of something that maybe really happened. I mean, kids dying.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, they happened. Yeah, maybe happened. That's one thing that I know is true because I even know where the place was. It was for mama come from, this territory where mama come from. And I don't know, they had many children, but I don't know why they had, they had no ground, land or anything. And they had many, and they were so, it was so hard for them. I don't know what he did. And my uncle, he had our home, mama's home place, he bought that. And the girl, his mama and her sister had to go milking in the summer to the quiet out for, they had the cows there. And my uncle always said, you go in and pour milk for those people. If the big, they took bread with them. And all that, so I know the place and all that. So, them it was awful hard for because they were hungry. They couldn't go to school, mama said, kids couldn't go to school in the winter because they didn't have any shoes. And it was weird, I don't know what did he do. No, I don't know, I don't know. Yeah, I know why it wasn't so much harder. Well, he probably done the little work for them farmers, you know, and you know, there could be very much the reason. He used to go to the farmers, but you know, they didn't hire someone. They didn't hire them, they didn't pay them. So, all that, I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, did many, do you think many people gave them, gave them food and help?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh yes, and that my uncle, that was big boy there. So, that was it, yesterday. That was one thing they had, they were going to go in and pour milk for them there. And then the mama said, always when we bake, we took bread with us. So, I tell you that was something mama was waste with. That they should do something for the... No, it's some. But, it's funny, mama was telling that the kids got big and went to America. And they one by one, and one helped the other to come over and they had, and they send home money to the parents. They said that the parents is going to know that they have kids and the kids, I would think the kids would have been really disgusted because it was a short-term job, food. But no, the parents got really good when they got older because the kids helped. I have gone with them by that place many, many, many times. And so, because of Andy, my uncle had, in the summer, he had that place away quite a ways from home. And the girls had to go there and do the milking and come back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that common to send money back to Sweden, to the old country, to the parents?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: They did, many did. Many did. Oh, they did, the boys did, they did. Mama told that there was a good dose, boys. Well, the many did, you know, see. And many were married and then they send home money, see. Well, you get three crowns and seventy-two. Of course, I didn't mean... Send, whatever you call it. I didn't mean their money, so they... One dollar, you see. You make it, you make it the dollars, it would be three, seven...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The money was worth a lot more. Yes.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: They didn't get rich, we know that, but it was so they could buy and have it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you came over here, did you figure you were coming over here for good, or did you think you were coming over here just to see what it was like?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, I really... I don't know, I think I used to went over here to find more work to do here. And also all the brothers came home and they said that if you like to work in the woods, there are all kinds of works around here, you see, in a deadly event, see. And I don't feel any way to go back to stay, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't think about that?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No. You know, you come over here, all new people, and you can't talk, nothing, and they're pretty hard. They really is, you know. Ah, yes. They know people. There's this nice yesterday you tried to... I work in the woods, never a little bit different than home in the woods. Oh, it's fine. Whenever you work alone, when you say, hey, we had a partner, see, maybe... When I come in, when I've done a lot of work, I come in the Swedish, you know, when I work, the company, more and more work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you got here, what did you think of it when you got here?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, I really don't... Well, you know, I heard about America too, and here around Troy, you see, for Anna's brothers, you know, things like that, and the many from... not from my place, but from Anna's, you know, here, you know, like. And there, after a lot of work here, and people were a little kind of friendly, a little more friendly, but poor people. I couldn't meet them, you know, bosses too, you know, that was nice. And they, when they say, you see, after once I come over, you can talk, and you kind of... Probably don't do the work so right either, but the bosses don't fire you either, no? Really, they can talk to you, and you see, after you like to work, and do the work, you know, they keep you. Oh, yeah. I worked with one boss there for many years. Who? Clyde, his name was Clyde Ratcliff. I worked with him many times. They don't pay really so much. Some other bosses, like now, you go into Elk River, and you go... Yeah, from Elk River, we took the basin, we call, they were steep ground, you know, and they paid more. Really did. What in 1929, in World War II, they made more money. And they were going to steep the ground working, you see. They made more money, really did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, when you got here, you finally got here and saw what it was, was it like you thought it was going to be, or was it different from what you expected?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, I don't think so, no. See, I worked one mountain town here, was sensing water pipes, and then I talked to my boss about the boots, I make more money. And he said, what about that? Man, no. And I worked for, I think, for over five years for a Potlatch lumber company, till the Depression came, see. Then I worked for gyppos, you know, and so on, you know. Well, I think they were really nice for you, see, in the recombinant camp here. All the recombinutions closed and washed up. Well, then you don't have to cook your own food, you know, nice and nice, good food, you know, and things like that. And then we put up the lunch then in the morning, and before breakfast we always stayed, you know, even to guard, you know. Used to live in Deary, you know, I worked with him many times, he was not the sweet one. He was nice, really nice, boss. So, that's it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there still many of the old lumberjacks, the old time lumberjacks, when you came here?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, there's too many left. Well, they had to say made some, then there might be some.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe in those days, they were mostly not, they were gone a lot, a lot of them were gone by then.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, well, you see, they come over here, they probably was many, some probably 50 or so. And you see, so when, and then they did that, you know. You know, in Spokane, they were sitting around the table, they were in the International Hotel, they were around the table, they were in the lobby. They were in the whole room, so we used to work on paper. So, I know they didn't talk with you. Probably a few months after, so they come up here and most of them were dying off. I don't think I can see one, probably one from a student school, he might be probably a couple years older than me. And I was up there, I asked a lady, but one fella, and she said he died. And he was able to die.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Malker Anderson?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, I worked for him. I worked for his brother Axel, dude. Have you seen Malker Anderson lately? Malker Anderson. Oh man, look at him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I saw Arthur Anderson last summer.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: He is old, but my heart happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I saw Axel Anderson. Doing Spokane. Yeah, in Spokane.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, I suppose he still lives. Yeah, he's still a big man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were they very different from each other, Axel and Malker?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, they was. Malker, he paid more. He paid more than his brother did. And he get up probably more logs too, you know. But then they have to pay for the company, what can't they pay? No, no, we were gyppoing, you know. But I know they have worked for Malker. And I said to him, you get stripped, you know. And the stripper used to put a ton, and I asked him how much are you going to pay now after here? Well, he said how much do you have? I got the dollar, I suppose he had a dollar twenty-five. Oh, you said I'll give you a dollar forty from the start, he said. We were used to but done with the strip. He gave us fifty cents a thousand right here. Then we had the saw boss, you know. I know him from the camp around here, and he was up there. He said, you don't get so good timber now. He said, we're not going to pay more. How much are you going to pay? I paid a dollar forty. Well, that's what we had. Then you had a dollar forty. Yes, but Malker said we're going to get a dollar forty. Well, if he gave you a dollar forty, I gave you a dollar forty and I gave you eight hours a day extra. Maybe four dollars, that was fifty cents an hour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Eight hours a day extra?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, and about four dollars, you say, about fifty cents an hour. Then we get, and then we get a dollar forty thousand for the strip, you know. He was good, though. Really good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did you think of Malker as a foreman?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, he was a good foreman, and the company liked to have him. And the only thing with him, he thought, drink. He drink with a working man, see. And some always took whiskey into him, you know. And that he done, and he get fired in 1932. I was up in the camp then. Well, in 1934, the superintendent hired him back. And that went good. He was married to a sister to Charlie Jelber. Yes, he was. And Naugle, the superintendent, told Charlotte, go talk to him, he said. He start drinking now again, and it's not good, you know, to drink with a working man. He couldn't drink weekend, there was no bother, but he couldn't drink in a weekend. Man, he was stubborn, too. Charlotte told me, I just could have told him, just could have told him, Malker, about it, that he gonna get fired. Well, he probably get mad, even if he get mad, you can have told him that. He get fired if he keep on, and he get fired.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Charlie tell him he would get fired?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, he never did. Charlotte said to Charlotte, you should have told him about Malker, you brother-in-law, that if he keep on, he gonna get fired again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why didn't he tell him?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I don't know why he said that. He said, I should have done it. Yeah, we should have done it, I said. So he don't get fired. Then he get fired, you see. And then he have to work, like other, you know. He was a good man, they would do common work, though. And he did. Then, here in 1942, remember, or 41, then they hired him back. There was a new superintendent, well, they get him to be a cat boss, you know, for them cats, yeah. And well, he know everything, till he was here, yeah. Then they took him to the camp boss again. When there were different superintendents, normal he died, see. Well, they went pretty good, first, you know, when he's drinking again. And there was out of Clarkia, a camp 40, well, he was drinking, so he never signed them checks, even. His camp boss signed them checks, you see. So his brother Axel had to go sign them, see. And he get fired, and then I ask one fella, and his name was Charlie Sandborn, I think it was. Was he drunk? No, he was rummed up drinking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was what?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Rummed up. He don't know nothing about what he do, he don't know, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That means he wasn't drinking?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, he was drunk, but his mind don't work right. So he never signed them checks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was that? Because he drunk too much already?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, he been drinking couple of every day, see. And that way, you see, and then I saw him in Elk River. I work here, I work north here on Camp 38, that one. And I was up in Elk River there, and he took us up there to fix up the estate, you know. I went with the one from Sweden, you know. He died not long ago here in the farm. He was in a restroom.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a Hagbaum?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, a Hagbaum, and Albert Norlen was his name, maybe. We were shooting stumps and rocks, you see. And then I talked to one fella, some community camp, was he drunk? No, he wasn't drunk, he was roamed up, he said, roamed up, see. Well, then we get down there and we have a center up there in Elk River, and fix up what is to stay. And I saw a man up there, and I talked to him a little bit, you know, in a tavern, he didn't like to talk, you know, you see. And then right after he killed himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he say anything to you when you talked to him?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, well, he answered me. Yeah, a little bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he seem depressed to you?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, he did, he never looked down, you see. I guess that drinking after that. He couldn't, I think he couldn't quit. I think he drink too much, he couldn't quit. Many years, they said, I talked to one other up there, Lee Mallory, he used to live in Deary, he was the kind of boss, if he'd undertaken the road boss, you know. Then he go up the roads and things, you know, and then he, he killed himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard that Malker really knew how to get the work done in the woods.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, you bet, he know. And he paid to, you know, get the men to work. He had most of them, sweet work, you know. He had some, some, some, some, you know, some, he took some, some, he went out to the roads, you know, and had a crew mate to make roads, you know. But Steve, you have to have a kind of roads that the horses can get into the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were working for him in the 20s and the 30s, when he was... No, 29. Yeah, when he was drinking then, did he drink every day?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, that time he don't drink. No, I never see him drunk in there. They don't have the whiskey in the camp either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you said, you said that the work, he drank with the working men.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, he did drink with the working men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When he would start drinking, would he drink all the time or what?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, I think he did, isn't he, when his keep on. But I know that in Camp 14, they all been parked, you know, they go through park up there. Well, 1932, worked little bit up there, worked a month up there. Well, you know, they were drinking so bad, them teams, they took a yerga whiskey, they were moonshine that time, you know. Well, they had a big fire and they lay behind the windfall and they're super tender. Come and they was drunk, so they haul them in with a truck. Took the team in and, well, the whole group get fired and Melky get fired at the same time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he drinking with them?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, he had too, you know, so he. I remember one morning, you know, he used to eat, you know. He took off by the camp, you know, they cook cars and had the yerga in the roots, so he had to have a drink before he couldn't eat. So, that was no good though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No. Well, this time when the teamsters were drinking, was Malker right there with them drinking?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, I don't think so. The sawballs and then Nogel, you know. Well, Nogel were there for every day. He was watching, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Watching Malker?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, so them drinking men too, you know. Couldn't get no logs out and drinking so bad. I was up there and asked to work and I don't see Malker, you know, see. And he said, I think Malker like to have some more men here. So, the next morning I get one with me from here and he went up and I talked to Malker. The user was up here? Yeah. Well, then he come after, he come in after supper.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: And there he did, you know. You know naugels were around then, you see. So, they were too bad. They wanted a good man like him. Men. Axel too, but he was a boss for many, many, many years until he retired.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Axel was kind of an overall boss, wasn't he?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: He was kind of a worker boss. He was over the mob, you see. He was closer to the superintendent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like as a boss? What was Axel like as a boss?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, he was okay, you know. He don't pay so much. So, Malker did. But then he actually, you know, the camps he up there in the basin, he had the whole bunch of day workers, you know. All the people, you know, he didn't pay them too much, you see. Worked by the day. Malker, you know, that was all Jepos, you see. And young, young Swedes, you know, in Norway, up and down the hills.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the work that you did in the woods mostly here?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, I cut logs in the winter. Then in the summertime in 1928, we made railroads for potlatch. They were kind of north from here. Camp 38, they called. And then after that, the Depression come. I used to work for one fella in Damen, there in Moscow. And then I worked for East Fork, Albert Lewis. Work on a green chain and a load of lumber. And I pile lumber sometimes, you see. Did you know Albert Lewis? No, he died in Spokane. And he didn't have a big business, so then? No, he sold out the potlatch. Yeah, definitely. He had a lot of money. Then somebody said he was going to have a broken night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard that you were strong.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: At one time I thought it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's what I heard. I heard that Oscar was very strong.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, that's what I thought. He was young then. Heavy, he was heavy then. Oh, I get bored.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember any bad accidents in the woods?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah. I hooked for Rauch, you know, had a farm, as you know. And once I'm driving a truck, log truck. And he get killed right, like the log hit him in the head. He killed him like this. And they be there to run for pain. So, he's on the road, he kind of a real big road boss, you know. Because he had to go to put Parson in Deary and ask him what he was going to do, you see, and things like that. That was his dad, you know. And I was in Oregon, 1932. And there was a nephew to the superintendent, he get killed. You know what's now. It happened more days, all the season. A young boy, he worked with him in 20 years old. I saw him in the van, a railroad car, you know, took him into the camp. He sent him to Astoria, he was little, like, and they called Portland to get a special man, yeah. Specialist, and they couldn't do nothing, and they called in the head here. They did. So. And I was home in 70. That's a pretender, you know, his name was Julius Jansen. And he was in a hospital living in Calsta. In Calsta, there. And he died there. So, I, my brother wrote, I said, I wrote, I know what he was there, you know, I could have went to see him, you know, but I worked for him in Oregon. So, he died. He got married here, and he had six boys, you know. And they went in, in loving too, them boys, some of them. They went to California. I see his wife, you know, so he told me in the camp, you come over home, he said, in the evening, you know, I think, and talk, he said, and so on, and so on. And then she had coffee, you know, and cookies. Small brew and oh, she'd come. So, she had many lumba, you know, she had to cook for. He used to work in the same camp as my brother, and he was here. So, he knew him. Do you know why that lumba, you had name come? I don't know, he, he don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, a jack means, like a jill, a jack could be a man and a jill would be a woman. So, lumberjack could be a...

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: A tail, a tail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A jack, a jack working in lumber.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Is that jack? Right. You know, a long time ago, the lumberjacks, they shipped out from Spokane. Probably those in the North over, but now they're home people, they're working in it. It's not a lumberjack, it's a lumberjack.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of the money that you were getting here, working in the woods? Did you think that the pay was good, or did you think they were trying to give you as little as they could, or what?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, I think they paid pretty good. Yes, they really did. Of course, the money here was much more than money in Sweden, you see, so if you figure it in in Sweden. But we couldn't figure it in in Swedish money, so long as we were here. No, but I mean, if you figure, if you had so many dollars and made it into Swedish money, that was a good feeling, it was quite a bit. Many did, you know, they set up money and they went home and they could buy a place, you know, and things like that, you see. And most of them doing bijaktam, they even married and single. They come into town, you know, then they holler timber in the tavern, you know, and drink like to beat the band.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And drink like what?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Drink like to beat the band and drink so long as they had money. Then they went back to the camp, you know, oh, that feels so good, then they were all right. Well, they also gave little money, then they started kicking about the grub, about the food, there was nothing right, you know. Then they took off for spook and, you know, they come back then, they were so quiet, everything was all right. That's where they kept on. Thousand dollars for them, they were probably one week or two, that was odd. They don't drink it, they steal from them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They steal from each other?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, some, them mix up with some other, both language, you know, time they had moonshine and tavern man and probably some girls too. And they get broke, then they come back and they feel so good. They ask them how they hope we had a very good time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Married men would do this too?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh yeah, they don't date them, they had a wife in Sweden, you know, and probably in Norway too and they drink up the money. They send some home and many went back though, they really did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Back to the old country?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, they put money here and send home and they went home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what about you Oscar, what did you do, did you save the money?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, I don't drink it up though. No, you see, I went over here after loan money from, I had some from her brother, see, and then in 28 I sent for her and the girls, you know, and I don't have the money, see that, two trips to pay for it. And so, that was it. And then the depression come, when we made it here, same place, we bought the place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year did you buy this place?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: In 1931, with the bet on it, see, and I was the only bet. They told me all kinds of stories to us that knew about America, when he went there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean all kinds of stories?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: What do men do? Negotiators, you know. When they come to this country and all that. No, they might be in big cities, you see. Might be what? In big cities. They say that I go and drink, you know. You're drinking, but other things, you run around with women and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were teasing you.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I don't know, I think they warned me. I think they did, some of my friends. Warned me and I work all the time. But I didn't take that so seriously, because I had to. Oh, there was some ladies that have been here, you know. But I heard that song about the kids that started here and not... But it seems weird, if that's the trouble. No. Man, that was a lot of work. Yeah, I know. Oh, how do you live or cool? I come to this country and I liked America.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you like it right away?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I wanted to come to America, I don't know why, because I guess it was right in us kids. Because so many of us were in the country. There were things too, you know. The working class of people back home, a long time ago, there was the merch, you know. The big ones, the big shops, rich people. Men, you work and they don't pay merch, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're saying you kind of grew up with the idea of America.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I don't know the boys wanting my dad was there and the boys was here. To me, I liked to come here and I liked it. So, I guess that was good for me, otherwise I could have had it pretty tough here if I should have. If I should have had a, if I ever had in my mind I should have everything because I was in America and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't really believe that?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, I didn't. I didn't expect that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you came over here, were there still a lot of people coming from Sweden or had it kind of trickled off?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh yes, it was in many of the dead times. Oh no. I don't know. So, when I came over here to Troy, except me, Torfin Vein and you know him, he came in for here but he was Norwegian. That's why I've been coming over here. Here we can say I'm from 1800 and ADN up to 1800. Axel Flodin came, didn't he? I think he was here before then when we came. He worked around here a little bit and then he went to Clement Falls and I think he's still there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, most everybody who was going to come had come already?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh yes, most of them they say come. But they come in 1886 and so on, homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was I thinking? When you came, did you think you might go back when you first came or did you think it was for keeps?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I didn't mean for keeps but it never dawned on me that I was going back. I mean, I felt that I should go back either. I don't know. Maybe if... now, Hjalmar, my oldest brother, Troy was his way of life. Poor thing. When he went back and forth all the time. Yes, and Troy.
