speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That must...

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: When someone could tell him about Troy, that was his life. In 1923, it was a different state, home and work. And there were many that were here in America some more days in that camp. They were married and they were back and forth and went home then. They started getting old and buying a place and had a family. There were many of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Sweden?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, in that camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Working for logging?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, I worked for a long time. I thought it would be hard for him to get a job. If I could. He had been here many years. He probably had some money. A long time ago, you couldn't make so much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you must have heard people talk about America all the time.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, they talk about America in the camp and all the time I heard that. Even the road home, you know, these are here. Yes. But if I should have... When I come here, my one brother I had in Troy, lived in Troy. But if I had gotten a place in Troy to live, then in town, it would have been different. But I didn't... Oscar had talked to Eric Nelson. I could live in the old house there out in the country. If I should have sat there, I'd have known. But then Hilda and Eric lived in the new house, so it worked out just beautiful. When you know here, if you've been buying a house in Troy, and the depression comes, we've been starving. Well, who can I give us? They work on WPA. They work around here. They took them from Troy, you know, in a truck, and they gave them dollars a day. Therefore, then you have to feed yourself. I was a citizen here. I couldn't get that woman. Why even a little? They've been sending us back to Sweden.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, so what happened when the depression came? Well, we were here.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: And we had cattle, we had pigs, we had chickens, so we were starving. And then I caught wood and had some to help me, so in the fall I sold wood to the NSC farmers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that's how you got by in the depression? You sold wood. You couldn't work in the woods then?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, that was it. I worked in the woods in the winter. A little bit in the summertime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I can see that. You would have had a rough time if you didn't have the land to live.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, you bet. We lived in town there. I don't know how you could have made it. They get work, you know, they're somewhat citizen. What's more citizen? The money we had probably had been buying the house. No, rent, it was to keep everything then. Yeah, you see that, we had work, you know, and we had a few dollars, you know, they didn't last. It was never talked about even, so that... We were talking about one time we should buy a house in Troy. So the woman, there was a mother to Mrs. Belkis, and she was the only if you understand the house, right off the border there, actually. And then she was the woman president, so he called up what she would say. And that was a good thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you get this place for when you bid on it? 3500.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: You see, that was depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that 160 acres? Yeah. And the house?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I think I get that if I sell it now. But isn't that when we think about, but yes, I think much land, you know, there wasn't much to a house there, and that I mean that, you know, even with that little amount of land... There are many that like to buy it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sure, yes.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Like to get away from... But I was glad to move here. You're paying an awful price. I was glad to move to my own home. Were you? Yes, it was nice, but there couldn't be better when I lived in the little house there, because for me to come and couldn't talk or anything, it would have been pretty rough. But we lived in the little house and they built a big... And we went back and forth. So that was really the best way. Well, I worked in the summertime with the president of the government.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often did you get to see him during those first years?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, many decided to come home. Oh, yeah, we was home. I was grading with the railroad north of here. We quit with noon Saturday, all the time. We worked in, we were gyppoing, we couldn't have did. And then we worked in half an hour in the last five days, you know. Then Saturday we went home with noon, so we never even ate. We were evolving, you know, at home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you have to be back Sunday night?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, we went back Sunday night. One time we went back, I don't know, the one time we went in the morning, we came back late, so we went to the woods already. Yeah, that was... I knew that was what it was about, Andy. But we made pretty good, well, we went to the cot logs. It was eight hours together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that you were lonely sometimes, Anna? Were you lonely sometimes?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No. No. Not directly lonely. No, I never, some people, you said some people cried and everything, but no. Beth, if I should have sat there all by myself, and Eric's having lived there, nobody lived out here, I don't know what I should have done, because when Eric took the team, I went to town. Either I went with him and got what I needed for grocery, too. And he'll die now. They couldn't buy it on Saturday afternoon, they'd get home. That's the way it was. They bought the Model T car, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you had a Model T? It looks like it was good for you to become friends with them, with the Nelsons. That was very...

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: It could have been better, because like I say, that I and Ingrid have sat there all by yourself, but I think I'm being scared to be alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How were you related to them?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Ingrid? How did you relate to Eric? Oh, yeah. I hear support. It is the distance on Mama's side, I can't exactly...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it's not too close.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, no, no. It isn't close. I think maybe my mother and Eric's parents or something were cousins or something. It wasn't close.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did it take before you were starting to feel that you could...

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Not every word, but maybe what they talk about, that doesn't take too long. And then Ingrid and the girl started to talk. Oh, you should hear her. She started to talk to Caesar. Two years old. The girl, she was with somebody, you know, a girl, and there she talked. You know, Amy Pearsons went... Yeah, where you live now, you see. And Ingrid met and Ruth was older and Ingrid was a bit afraid, but it sounded... She rebelled and talked and I guess they didn't understand. She was afraid to learn faster when they didn't care.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, could she speak English pretty good as a child? Did it take her a while to learn?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, after she met those kids around there, she learned, but I didn't say it was the best. She talked just as good some days when we were born here, but they don't talk really good. Oh, yeah, now. Yeah, when we went to school, they had no trouble. Many of us when we talked to, you know, we started school, we didn't know nothing English. They were home and parents never talk to Americans. I can't see none of that. But Ingrid's teacher was telling me they went down to Birtwick and she come one morning, kind of excited, it was after Vernon was born. And she said, I can't, you know, she said, I can't talk better English and my brother and he's born here. All he could was to scream. Her teacher told me that not so long ago. You know, she was the teacher. She told me that not so long ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, I've heard that a lot of the old timers from the old country who had come over, especially the wives that didn't have a chance to get out and mix much, they never did learn to speak English.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: That's the way it is when now, Lois, Vernon's wife, she went to Swedish school in Spokane and I can write to her and it's nothing for her. Ingrid had been taking up Sweden in Spokane too. Oh, yeah. She said, I thought I know Sweden and they were different. Yeah, she thought like we do. That doesn't good Swedish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's not good Swedish?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, that doesn't perfect like we read it in school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About Chuck Wells. You knew Chuck Wells?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, yes, yes I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about him?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: You know? I probably told you before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I don't know but if you did tell me again.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, we went and we were down over here, north of the highway. We went to our river and... Yeah, we'll be with the cookers. And fix up what we should stay and Chuck Wells give a bet, you know? So, and when noon we put money together so we bought a quart of whisky, see? And we drink a little bit, you know, then and we give to Chuck Wells. He's an awful cold. And we went home, we bought one more and the boss was wet so he put money in too. So, he gave him, you know, wet hot no, we gonna give him, he drink, you bet he did. The boss? No, Chuck. Well, I have an awful cold.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Chuck didn't put any money in?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, no, he put no money in. Well, he live on the side there, he go to Helmer and they be on the west side of the highway. I think it will be, north of the highway will be. And he walk up, they be in other lunch pocket in his hand and he walk like that all over the road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He weaved around.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, we left, you know, then he start to sing, he start to sing sweet. Yes, you bet he start to sing sweet and talk sweet, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could he do that good?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, he done pretty good. No, he did. So, in the day, the day when I was a person, one time my son-in-law went, you know, when I started to talk sweet to him. And don't you think he answered me and my son-in-law said, the first time I heard a nigger talk sweet. He said, but not goes too far, he said. Not goes too far. That's all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was Chuck, you know, did you think that Chuck stood up for himself and for his rights? I mean, he was negro and I just wonder whether he, what he thought about if he got much prejudice.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, I don't know, I never heard he said nothing. But he, that way he come over here, in West, he work for them people. And the name of Wells, you know, and that he took the name, they give him that name, you see. The most people, the nice people he work for. The white people, I suppose. And that way they see. And you have read a book about him, have you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've seen The Trees Grew Tall.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, I've seen that, you see. I think it's a good book. Two brothers, Rowian, Chuck and Mary. Right. And we had a lot of, he was a nice, though. But it was the King, the King family that said, I wasn't there, I wouldn't have been with them, but he said that King family that lived in the, people that were, what it was just like, they were all same people. And the boy believed, they said that when he went to school and the girls there in Sweden. They were Mary's kids, she was Mary the one named King. And he, in the First World War, he went to France, I think, come and never come back. You don't know what happened, honey, if he gets killed in a war and he done something to kill him. I don't know, but I heard that they said that they were treated nice. Oh yeah, there was same some nice people, you know. It was a family from Diggory. Mrs. Berglum was a tea, she died first. You know, we were in Nora Church at the funeral and the colored people came and was at the funeral. I wonder who it could be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that from Mary?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I don't know. No? I don't know who the funeral was. I thought it was Mrs. Berglum. Berglum. Could be. I think it was Mrs. Berglum and they were dairy people and they come to them. Oh, that must seem some white people. I remember because we were dairy in the church. And when they had to learn Swedish, it was all Sweden, Norwegians and Moron. And they talked, you see, they couldn't talk English then. They were just talking. So they were not speaking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you first came over, I'm curious about how you thought the community here, the neighborhood, compared to the way it was in Sweden, in the neighborhood you'd been in there.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: The people here were the nicest it could be. Yeah, that way. Because at home, you have that scare when you were nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Over there you mean?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Over in Sweden. When you know here, the people live close together, you see. Here is far between for the bigger, more land here, you see. Like that, I come from, that was pretty close where you come from. Yes, neighbors, you know. They were kind of closer here too. Now we come over here and say now there's a big farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel as at home here, people were as friendly here as they were over there?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, they were. Oh, they were friendly here, I thought, because I expect I was in America and I would be so high and mighty so they wouldn't talk to me. No, I thought, and I liked America from the beginning because people were nice and friendly. Oh, they were nice and friendly. So, I liked. No. Man, they were closer than neighbors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it was kind of similar here to the way it was back there, the way people, the way the Swedish people were? I'm wondering about how being in America changed the Swedes who had been here for a while. I mean, were they very different, having lived here for 34 years and then Swedish people were back in Sweden? Had they changed?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, I don't think people, I don't know how they would be from the beginning, the American people, I don't know that. But I couldn't say that there could be any difference, but of course we were among the Swedish birds there. You know, they talk Swedish, it was just like home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you see, what I'm thinking is here most of these people had left Sweden, right? They left 30, maybe 30, 40 years before you did. And I'm wondering how being in America changed them as compared to what the people were like in Sweden. You see what I'm asking? It's kind of a hard question, I know.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I think they changed, you know, they changed songs, you know, they did. They didn't come from the 30, 40, 50, 60 years ago, we had a bus. They were thanking, they were thanking in Sweden, it was exactly the same way the time they left there. See, when they were deafened, they were deafened, they went up and off the things, and they changed a lot of things, and they had done it here too, you see. And they come from here, home, and we see it, you know, the people over there, they couldn't see that they were in America. Oh, we all, we could always see they come from America. They didn't walk right the same, they were back home in Sweden.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How different?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, they didn't. In one way, they walk faster here. They go faster, you can see right on the sidewalk here in town, they go faster, you know. No more. Eric Nielsen's brother was here in 1959, he was in Moscow there, and I walked, he said, you walk so fast, I can't keep up with you. Eric Nielsen's brother was also a woman, he come to this country. He said, he stopped there, and more rush over here, but same with work and everything. How old was the older man? Oh, I think he was 69. And Eric, he talked Swedish, but he forgot, he said, neighbors. Neighborhood, he said. But, the neighbors, and only he couldn't get that neighbor, he didn't know what it was, he said, now I say that never heard of this. That was not, that never heard. Neighborhood, Eric said neighborhood, you see. Now he say that. Now he come with that word again, he said, never heard, then Mark heard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Never heard?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, never heard.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Never heard.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Now he come with that again.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: How old were they?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were they more, once they been in America, were they more independent, do you think?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, I think they was, yes. They more independent, see. And they more to go ahead over there, you think like that, they some been home there all the time, they haven't been out, you know, they, it's not like they some come from here. Never heard. They little difference, you see. But people long ago. They go and travel too, you know, they see. And people long ago come from home and come here and to homestead and all that, they had to be, cannot get up in them too, otherwise they couldn't have got anything. They had to work, they had to, cannot ride, they were with the language and everything to make it. I never thought about it because, and I come here because we never had taken anything, but like Eric, when he came here and took that land there, I wonder how he did it. Hmm? I wonder how Eric Nielsen did it when he came here and took that land and... No, he bought that land. Oh, he bought that land. At first he was, he was run, he was run home. Then he was in Missoula first and then he was run home. Republic he was. There he crossed the Canadian border and then he come around here. Oh, and that's the difference of the... And he stayed in Moscow first where he had an uncle there. No strength there. Yeah, yeah. And then he come here and bought. I think they paid $450 for it. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you say they, they thought that things hadn't changed in Sweden since they'd been there.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, they were, they were, they were thinking the same way, you know, the whole fellas on the left, that they were the same, over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But things were changing in Sweden too.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, there was. You bet. They think they were the same, you know. There was an, Anna's brother, there at the valley of... of home in 70. Well, he was used to but they live only a short. And they thought that Troy was the same. No, I said, if you come over here you probably don't know nobody and you don't know them, them fields even. The difference, a big farmers and many houses down you'd be surprised. You don't even know the highway, the new highway. And when they thought about the same... No, no, no. But he liked to come, he liked to be over here again, but back and forth all the time, you see. America was his life. You see, in Troy, you know, when I said you'd be surprised. He fought for America too, so he...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the war? Oh yeah. And his wife just didn't want to live here.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, she didn't like to come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gosh, I can't figure out why she wouldn't want to come here.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I can't figure out that either. I mean, farms are there, and here ever. No, America was his.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because you didn't mind coming. Why would somebody mind coming?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, I felt it was what I should do. I never talked about that. No, I felt sorry for them. They said, they wrote and said that if they told them, and they got older, and even forgetful, that if they start talking about Troy, then he woke up. Oh yes, when they mentioned Troy. Yeah. And he was here so much around here. But anyway, he never forgot that. When he was young, he came over, you know, and kind of raised with the mother, the boys here, and things like that. Ah, so he...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he tried pretty hard to persuade her to come?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, I don't know. I don't know. I don't know if she was afraid. He went back here the last time he went back in 1931, in the spring. He was here a couple of years the last time. He was married then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say, you think she was afraid?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she ever try it? Did she ever come to try it?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, I don't think so. Her mother was living, you know, and she wouldn't leave her, I suppose, first. But they had a neighbor girl there, you see. Her parents was... Yeah, that girl was born here, even in this country. But, yeah, that was hard. I tell you, it was really hard. That mother had... All she had was her dad at home and so he had a little house, a few acres around it and all that. And he wrote and said that when I used to have one child and they don't come to see me, and just kept on like that. And she felt bad about that too because she needed to go, was going to go and see her dad and she did. And there they had three kids, Elson and Dorothy and the other boys named Almon. Yeah, they come there, you know. There was no money in that. It was pretty hard for her. She had cowards, cowards too and all that. But it was not income from that little place her dad had. But she said she can't leave him. She'll go back because that would, for one thing, he was old and she knew he eventually will die and all that. But it was pretty rough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's Hjalmar's wife. Hjalmar's wife's mother.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: That was the neighbor girl I was going to tell you about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, the neighbor girl. I see.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: And that Elsa, you see, now when Hjalmar was the oldest, she is, was so crazy. So she had all plans for Hjalmar. She was, she said, we go. We take a plan. We go and then we come over this place. Of course they come to visit us. Then the airplane go right down. Then the airplane come right down here and then they went. Oh that Elsa was a crazy thing. That helped Hjalmar quite a bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why, just knowing that he might come back?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, that was, she was, talked a bit like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She'd been over in America.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, she was born here. That girl was born here. No, he was, she was back here too. Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she, did she ever get to come back here either?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, not the mother. They never come back. He went back. The man came back. The father. Yeah, it was just that, that of her, Elsa's mother. That was just that. Made her, the daughter feel so bad because she kept on writing and writing and all that. And they didn't, they lived back, they were in, back, they lived back east. They didn't walk again. They didn't walk again. And finally they talked about this sugar home. Yeah, they come home, there was no money there. That old man had no money. And there was that little place, wasn't any income. The only thing was that they got a cow, they had milk and things like that. And you know that the kids beginning to get, it took quite a bit even. And food was different. Yeah, the kids wanted so and so like they had hair. It was pretty rough for Marie because, and she thought she couldn't leave her there. But, just, her husband went back to make money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To America.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh yeah, come back here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she and John were, used to talk about America.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, I tell you that was life. Oh that crazy Elsa, she could help make up the world. But that helped live them up too. Yeah, because Troy, that was his life. It was really too bad that they couldn't be here. There was a good to him, there wasn't any hardship on him. But he was too much in Troy, so Troy was his.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was Stelmer's wife's name? Marie. Marie. Was she very close to her mother?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, I think she was home all the time. I tell you, yeah, of course she was home all the time. And if I tell you that, it's crazy John built a new home for her because he bought the place. And then Marie, his wife couldn't, she couldn't move to her own home, a new building. Because it would be too long for her to live, her where she lived before, and you could see the smoke. By her hair you could see the smoke a little ways away. Of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well if she couldn't move a quarter of a mile, I can see why she couldn't move to the United States.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, you can say that, because it wasn't more than a quarter of a mile. So that would have been, she told me when I was there, oh it was a lonesome to move over here and it was her new home. It was a lonesome to live home. She didn't come here, she hadn't been here anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She never had been here.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: If she had been coming over here, she'd been going back. Yeah, I don't know. I guess I can bet anything she'd been going back. But it was hard for John, he didn't complain, but America was his life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old was John the first time he came over here?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh I think maybe he was about 18 years old, maybe he was younger. Now he came here first time. First time he was ever 19, 5.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old was he?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well in 19, 5, and he was 87 and he died. And you can see... He asked how old he was when he came here. Maybe he could have figured it out then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year did he die in?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: He died last year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he was 87?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: It depends.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: And then he came here in 19, 5.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then he would have been 13, 18?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: 18 years old, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which you said to start with.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I thought that they'd think of us about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well Oscar, I wanted to ask you about why it was that you had to go to Norway to work in the logging? Why couldn't you work in Sweden?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well sometimes we didn't have work and here was the other thing. I liked to get away from home and I was more free, you see. And I think the Norwegian bosses were a little better. They liked to have Sweden work for the bus. Harold worked in the Norwegians. That's the difference. We worked in 1920. We out in the woods they had to pack food. And the Norwegians boys, they went home around Saturday morning, come back Monday. Sometimes they had girls with them, they stayed all week. Well they wouldn't work. And we stayed there all the time. I remember one time the wings, the beds, we couldn't work for two days. My brother and I, we went to work Sunday. Did you have a tent? No, we had a house and a cabin where I used to come in with cattle in the summertime. And girls came and took care of them. There was only girls and older ladies who took care of them. So that's when I got my house. And my boss called up, the big boss, he said we can't let them boys go. But then there would be nothing done here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well you say you have more freedom to get away from home. What do you mean by that?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: We come and done with the work. We stayed in a hotel and a cafe. And there we had the board where we bought probably a big meal a day. And then we had the lunch meals that we bought in the store, bread and cheese and stuff. And things like that. And then we went. But that day was more free. It was hard more to see that way. My brother used to be here, he was older. I had a Norwegian partner, he was about the same age. We were probably about a year older. So we worked together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work in Norway as much as you did in Sweden in those years?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I think I did. I think I was 17. I was 18. I went there 19 and then I worked elsewhere. I was 20, I was 21, I was there 23 and 24. In Norway? Yes, in Norway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would the season be you would work?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well in 1920, in the spring, and I worked there till Christmas. I was home once I think. Then I went back after Christmas and then I worked in the woods. Well I worked in the summer time too in the woods. And last part of October 1920 we went in and worked close to Oslo. I went to Oslo in time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were in the woods there, was it like a lumber camp like we have here? Or was it fewer people?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, there were a few of us and we did our own cooking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just how many in one place?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, that cabin we stayed in there was one, two, three, four, there was around five of us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And was that the only cabin there or were there a bunch of cabins?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: There were more cabins there than the others stayed in too, you see. There was, we call that, in the summertime, the cabin, you know. There were one or two more empty ones. Some that didn't work in the woods. But you know the way they lived. They had that fire here and they cooked there. They had an open fire. They cooked there and there were so many of it. A bit was hot many times. When you got to this road. 1921, there was a man in the camp, you know, we had a stove, you know, it was kind of hard to get something to cook, you know. And we were wet too, you know, very close. Where did all of you sleep there then? Was it more of a room? It was more of a room and we made a kind of brisk, what we call, you know, made of, I think we had lumber at that time. We made a day with, one, two, three, four hours and a half on that, you know. And then there was tapuks, you know. And we got lassie too. That was all for their work on the night on us. And I think there was a sweet summer home here. I'm pretty sure there was. I think there was once, our arms were home and here had a horse in the drove. Yeah, two. And he was home, I think he got the lass with him and he get all of it here with you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you still figured that it was more freedom than staying at home would be.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, it was more freedom, you know, than it really was. That winter was a development. I could not have went back to, close to us and work all winter. The boss told me, you come back and you get work all winter. I never did, you know, when I went up in Norway anyway. In logging camp, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean? You didn't work in the winter too? Yeah. We did.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I worked in the after.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, do you think Oscar you worked as hard, as hard in there in Norway as you did in America in the woods? Did you work as hard there as here?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, I think I did, you know. It was not different. Except here you don't have to peel them. Like you did there, you had a spud, you know, when you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you have to peel them there?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, they dried, you know. See, so they won't go and they had the log dried. And you see somewhere you can go to the barroom and if you peel them, you know, they dried them. We done that in Sweden too. But now they were home, they never peel them anymore. They haul them to the road and they take them with a truck now. Same from here. You see. You shame so.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Can you eat, you know, all kinds of good food and you don't have to wash dishes? Yeah. Cook, you know. I think I know Oscar was up working, where he was I can say. And he had his dishes with us from home. And that was all cold black except the peas here where he had his mouth. So if that was the dish washing it, I don't think that was working. Maybe sometime we washed dishes, you know. Yeah, the cob was black and here was that spot there where he had his leg. And suddenly, you know, there was something about the clothes. And then, not in the wintertime, in the summertime we did. And then we had fixed up the tools, you know, sharpen them up, you know, things like that. So you work these days. Now what happens? So America, my... Man, you have to sharpen your tools here except the saw. They had the saw if I live here. When I asked, you have to sharpen the axe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the smuggling that you did, you know, across the border? You mentioned that to me before. Was that tobacco mostly that you sold?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, there was gas and soap, you know. Hand soap and paper and things. You can buy that in Norway. Paper, but they didn't have paper. In Sweden, no, it was hard to get that in the winter of our time. And here in 1918, I built with help. And then we took snuff back to Norwegian. And they didn't have snuff there, you see. That we traded, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How careful did you have to be not to get caught?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, you went over in the night. Wouldn't it be nice if you were caught? If you come over to the Swedish side, you know, that was all right. When we went over to Norway, they don't bother us for the day, no. They like the head that snuffs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you go? Did you have to cross over in the countryside someplace?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, we did. We went over in the countryside, trailing the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you say you skied? Did you ski?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, the one time we did, the winter time, we skied over there. Six feet is small. We went left, 7.30 in Norwegian, and went over to a big lake, coming in Swedish side. Good skier.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that, did you think that was dangerous at the time, or did you think it was fun, or what?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, that was it. They were real. One way they were kind of fun, you know. We don't make much fun of them. We don't. Try bloody things, you know. One time I remember when we went, we wait in the Norwegian side, they let the costuming inspector go to sleep, so you can see they had light. And I went to sleep, you know, in the fall, you know. So he wake me up and said, we better go now. The man went to sleep, and I turned the light on. Now we had a lot of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tell me, you know, sometimes I've heard people say around here that there's a little competition between Swedes and Norwegians here, a little competitiveness, and I wonder if that was true back there. Was there any competition between Swedes and Norwegians?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe that's not the right word. I don't know if it is, but...

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: They used to come together, you know, on the dances and stuff like that, you know, and then they'd be drunk and they'd fight a little bit. They'd be drunk, you know. And then they'd be home, and they would never date, but race rights were in the board, you see. And Norwegians come over home and we went over there, it was the same people. And they come in, you know, they couldn't help it, you know. When I was on the dances there, I had no thinking of it. But now I think there were some of them. Well, they always know, you know, they're drunk, some Norwegians feed, you know, get involved, pet, you know. That couldn't happen. That can happen here too, though. The first time, in 48, when we were home, we went into Norway. I was going to buy something, a gift, for our son, and I wanted to ski sweater. I went there, we were in there, and I understood this clerk too, so I made it, but it was so nice. Oh yeah, I was in there now, and they were home too. I was there in 1920. But you see, the motive was so much difference in Norway, and American money more, and in Sweden, and American. I wrote two English just for fun, I said, I went, we're into Norway, and so I bought things, I bought a ski sweater for Vernon, and for Roger, yes. Because they were very cheap, they were hundreds, and so many. And I bet you fell over, over a hundred, but that was the Norwegian-American point. Now we paid 74 grand in Sweden. I did it just purposely, I knew, I was surprised. My brother was over there, Oscar and we, he went in especially, because you know Indian about, so. I did send it right over here. I could understand that man. He talked good, you know, that's the way in Sweden. Out in the country they talk support Swedish, and I guess maybe it was slang in Norway too, and he talked so nice Norwegian, this clerk. He was over there, they were home in 70, and my brother and my son were there, and they went to other place, and Roger and girls, you know, and he was a girl's wife. And they were kind of on this, I happened to see, at the sign, they have been selling beer, you know. I told my brother, we go in there and see, and buy you some glass of beer. Yes, we're gonna have two glasses. You know we have to pay a dollar a piece for it, a glass of beer. It cost us two dollars and maybe it was over seven Norwegian crown. So I told the girl, that was high price, I said, I talk Swedish. And in America the two bits, 25 cents, when she don't like it I could see the hand. Oh, did you say that? Oh, she did. In the other room there was a Norwegian sitting, she carried beer from the other side. Oh, even all the world. And I can see how you can sit and drink and pay seven crowns,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: you think she was charging you more?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, I thought it was an awful price to give a whole dollar for a glass of beer. You couldn't expect to like that. She would like it though. That's why I said, I give you 50 cents. That did sound. I had left, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, I want to ask you about something a little different, which is, I've heard it said that in the old country, that the women didn't have much freedom at all, that they were expected to do, you know, just kind of work for the men. And I've heard that said about the old country and that that was the old country way. Do you think that was true?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, you ask her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I now can ask you.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: For the women to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that they didn't have much say about things, that the men had all the say and the women did what the men did.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: That I didn't have any much experience with, because you see, I was young and the kids didn't have much to say in their home. No, no, not to hear me. Yeah, I say the kids, you see, I wasn't. You always heard, too, if it's a young man, the young that he can move with him. Oh, no, then I was. Then I was. But I guess it was more, oh, it was more, a long time ago, it was used also, the women had nothing to say. The women, you know, a long time ago, they couldn't even inherit even the money. That was really long. The oldest boy in the family, he get from the house. That's what it was. And the women, they were nothing. And they took care of the cattle, you know, and all that. And the farming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's also, I heard, the women did, you know, heavier farm work.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, they did. We did, yeah, it was that, the women.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But so when you were growing up, what was it like then?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, oh, it was hard work. No, you can tell how you work. You can tell how you have to work. Yeah, I say that, I can tell step for step, I don't remember. Took care of the cattle and you take care of the house. It was out in the field. Oh, yeah. And all the milking. Clean the house. So for the kids. It was all there was, I think. Oh, it was lots of hard work. Really, it was. But I was. We out and sprayed my door and in the fields.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But then, did the women have much say so about how things should be done?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, no, not that things should be done about that, I can say. Of course, I never had anything at home. I mean, when I was home, I used to, was the daughter in the house. It must have been some sad day. That must have been bad. But I had an awful good dad, so he was so good to mama and all that. You know, that day you have to work. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why? Because you don't work, so.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Oh, yeah, we sure had to work. That was nothing. No. But I have worked since I come here too, being out in the fields and in the barn. That was easy for me because I was used to that. People that had been used to it would have been hard. Take a young girl here, take the barn and you milk. Can you milk?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I never had to. Never had a cow. But was it very, do you think it was very different here, the position that women had in a family?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: You know, I come to Eric Nelson, Sandy Hilda, and we taught in the barn and she wasn't out in the field. But it was just like home, that she had to be in barns and milk. So, of course, it wasn't all places. There are a lot of women here, the Swedish women in Norwegian, they had to work in the barn too, you know, milk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about if you were going to decide how something should be done, like are you going to move here or are you going to take this job or how are you going to spend the money that you have? Was that a decision that the husband and wife would make together or would the husband make the decision all by himself?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what I mean?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I don't think that the husband won't decide all by himself, he has to talk it over. He has to.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Right.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Now, Eric Nelson was impossible too, huh? Hilda never liked that he went to sales and bought all kinds of junk and all that. But he did, and he didn't know until he had got someone to haul home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of things would he buy?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: You know, I mean, he bought, you know, if it was a farm sale, and there was things he didn't need, he nodded or something. Oh, he was so disgusted too, he couldn't be worse, but I never said that to people, relatives around here. He used to say that, I have rode against this dream so many years, he says. Yes. He talked to me about it, we were so good friends, her and I, and I never mentioned it. No, he was, of course, if it has been useful things, but I can't know if it was that what he bought and those sales he did. Well, he bought a lot of stuff, but he bought some good stuff that he had to have a farming to. Yeah, well, you know, then... Flowers and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you have this place pay off?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: It took me 11 years, I paid on it. Isn't that something? In 19... 35. 42. I paid on 1800, I was 17 lefty, and then the hard time come and... Well, you know, you have to buy stuff when you move with it, like cattle and things like that, chicken and... Pay very much, but that way. And I thought that was 1700 left, it wouldn't take long to pay, but I made it very good in the woods. Well, the depression come, I was in the world. And then there was nothing paid either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever, did you have to rent this place out to farm it or did you farm it yourself?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, that's how I lived here, I was a guy, kids, he farmed it one year after I bought it. In 1932 we moved here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then you farmed it?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: 32, no, he farmed it in 33, I think. I don't... One year after he moved out. But he asked if you farmed it. Yeah, then I started farming. Then I had to buy houses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work in the woods too or did you stop work?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, there was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was no work?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I worked in the summertime, the spring work, you know, and things like that. And that was the most work, you see, that's the most.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what happened in 36 when the woods started opening up again? Did you keep farming or did you go to the woods?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, I keep farming. I'm farming here to... What you did? To 1948. 1958.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that pretty much stopped your working in the woods then? You didn't work much then?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: I worked in the woods some, you see, and then I had to quit, you see. And then in 1948, that soul bank come in, you see, the government paid, you see. And there was infotan, yes, in 68. And then I rented out to Dan McKinsey. So I went to farming then. And I get rid of them horses, you know, and they bought a tractor, you see. Like that, you see.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: So?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yeah, it wasn't much different. It wasn't much different. They first come from Sweden, they were here. But I like America, of course, I always have. And when I was in Sweden I liked it there too. You know, here. We moved here and we worked for ourselves. We tried to make it, see. Yeah. They did different. You work home there and you help them to the modern day, you see. And take care of them. And here we work, try to make it and pay for the place. It will be different. I don't know. You work for yourself that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's a big difference.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Yes, it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In that way. Yes. But I think you're talking about, you're talking about the people and the two, that they weren't too different. I mean, the life itself was kind of similar here and there. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Like I say, I guess maybe I shouldn't have come to this country but I'm glad I did and I wanted to go and I went. And I love all of them. Yeah, I mean, there are a lot of work here but the same thing in Sweden. Oh yeah. There wasn't many. Some were born here and they work here often. They work in the barn and things help, you know. So many of them, three, they had ten, eleven kids. And twelve kids they had. If they've been living in town, they never can support twelve kids. And they have to be on the farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean here? They had that many kids here? No. Back in Sweden?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: No, you mean here. Here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Sweden they had smaller families, didn't they?

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, they had up to eight kids. Oh. No, years ago. Yeah, they had up to twelve. A long time ago they don't, but now they don't hardly have any kids since later years in Sweden. No, not in Sweden. They don't have any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they had bigger families here than in Sweden in the early days.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: Well, they had them, Sweden come over and took home state. They had a kind of big family. Unless it's different now but when we come home in 48 we are really surprised. Of course, in my home there were eight kids. Eight of them were home. There were eight kids. And here there were ten, eleven, twelve. And they made it. You know, get the land for nothing, you know, in the old British land and you're going to raise a family with eleven, eleven kids and twelve. I don't think you can make it. But they said... And then you had a breaking plough and two, three horses to break little bit, little by little, you know. But of course the women thought, they said, their health breaking and all that. No, they helped you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I guess...

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: ...saw down big timber, you know, and burn it off but it couldn't stand in the logs. I guess it was the Swedish men that had their wife out. I don't know if the American men had. Well, you know, you see, they saw down them big trees, you know, there was a crock-crock saw, they put in the hair, they put it underneath sand. And what, you couldn't hold it and get them down? Family lived over here, they get 40 acres, they clear 40 acres. Yeah, but what... Women and his wife, you know, are friend men. But who did they get that land from? They had to get land from the effective of the... No, they were land they said. No, probably they was harp and some. They cleared the land for the active of the flood in. And then they got 40 acres. Thank that for the work. And the work, you know, I don't know how much the work they done on my hill yesterday. Till I get the wagon. Yeah, I remember. When we come here and I remember we had time to get a shovel or whatever it was. Of course we didn't have any money. We got it, but I mean we had to figure. Oh, I thought a little bit and made them extra money and so it was good, you see. So we... But that is rewarding though if... When it is really hard to get things that we get at, can make it. That means more than if you take the money and go and buy it. We are so happy now. You try to make it, you see. There's a lot of difference. Man is not meant to get money in a thousand for nothing, you know. They don't appreciate either. And if you work and you get money over it, you say, do you work for that money? No, when we come home, everything goes alright. We get to see and so yeah, that's life. That one, that lived up to Hjalmar on the time when they were going to come in the plane and drop down here. And she said, we tell them, Hjalmar, we do that. He didn't leave.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We tell them we're coming to have...

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: To use drop down here so they didn't have to bother to have any. They flew right there where they were going to this town. Oh that Elsa is full of craziness.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She's still living.

speaker: Anna Bengston Thomason or Oscar Thomason
words: She's a young, last young girl. And I am an awful... And when Ingrid was little, she tried to have it because Ingrid was so good to talk to her, to little. And she told her to stay where there was. And her mother was a mad at her. She said she could have hit with anything you can talk to. And she didn't care. She told her, Ingrid was a little. She didn't understand what it was. But I surely didn't like it and I talked to her. Oh then she had some other fun. She said, I'm mad. She got her to say, no, honestly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I should probably get going because Laura is going to have dinner by now I suppose. She'll have to wait. She won't be able to eat until I come. Maybe she will eat. Who knows? I don't think she will. Maybe she will. I really liked it.
